merge_policy.threshold: merge documents when TF-IDF cosine similarity >= 0.60 OR 5-token shingle containment of the smaller document >= 0.60
merge_policy.why_containment_is_used: Containment catches shorter PDFs that are substantially embedded inside longer PDFs even when whole-document Jaccard/cosine is lower.

documents.title: www.additudemag.com/download/
documents.full_text:
www.additudemag.com/download/
E
motional outbursts are habitual responses to uncomfort-
able feelings. Since the ADHD brain is wired for intense feelings, anger and frustration often ambush when adults with ADHD underestimate their ability to cope with stressors. Metacognition is an awareness of your thought processes and patterns. This executive function can help you monitor and evaluate your thoughts and behaviors without judgment when experiencing anger, thereby changing your emotional reactivity. You can’t control stressful situations, but you can control your reactions by building metacognition skills.
Metacognition Questions
• What is happening to activate me or make me feel on edge (e.g., receiving negative feedback, being blamed unfairly, being chastised, or feeling shame)?
• What sensations do I feel when I become angry or upset (e.g., headache, stomachache, sweating, chest tightness)?
• What behaviors do I resort to when I’m upset or angry (e.g., lashing out, attacking, shutting down, catastrophiz-
ing, blaming, or denying)?
• How are others reacting to my words and actions? What are their faces or bodies showing? • What has helped me calm down in the past? (Make a list of coping tools and save them on your phone and on sticky notes around your home). How to Respond When You’re…
Mildly Uncomfortable
• Notice the negative, critical voice changing your thoughts. • Compare your limiting beliefs about the situation or yourself to the real facts. Activated
• Acknowledge and validate your feelings. Say, “It’s okay to be angry. But what can I do to settle down?”
• Repeat affirmations or supportive phrases like, “This is not the most fun or my best moment, but I’ll get through it.”
• Visualize yourself settling down. Picture yourself by a lake, tossing in rocks. As each rock falls, feel yourself relax into your feet or chair.
On High Alert
• Try triangle breathing: Breath in four counts; hold for four counts; exhale for six counts. Pause and repeat.
• Physically remove yourself from the stressor or situation.
• Engage in physical activity (e.g., walk, run, or swim).
• Turn to a timed distraction. Listen to music or play a game on your phone.
Copyright © 2023 by ADDitude, All rights reserved.
ANGER MANAGEMENT
Steps & Scripts to Use When You’re Fired Up
By Sharon Saline, Psy.D.

text_blocks.text: www.additudemag.com/download/
E

motional outbursts are habitual responses to uncomfort-
able feelings. Since the ADHD brain is wired for intense
feelings, anger and frustration often ambush when adults with
ADHD underestimate their ability to cope with stressors.

Metacognition is an awareness of your thought processes
and patterns. This executive function can help you monitor
and evaluate your thoughts and behaviors without
judgment when experiencing anger, thereby changing your
emotional reactivity.

You can’t control stressful situations, but you can control
your reactions by building metacognition skills.

Metacognition Questions
• What is happening to activate me or make me feel on
edge (e.g., receiving negative feedback, being blamed
unfairly, being chastised, or feeling shame)?

• What sensations do I feel when I become angry or upset
(e.g., headache, stomachache, sweating, chest tightness)?

• What behaviors do I resort to when I’m upset or angry
(e.g., lashing out, attacking, shutting down, catastrophiz-
ing, blaming, or denying)?

• How are others reacting to my words and actions? What
are their faces or bodies showing?

• What has helped me calm down in the past? (Make a list
of coping tools and save them on your phone and on
sticky notes around your home).
How to Respond When You’re…
Mildly Uncomfortable
• Notice the negative, critical voice changing your thoughts.

• Compare your limiting beliefs about the situation or
yourself to the real facts.
Activated

• Acknowledge and validate your feelings. Say, “It’s okay to
be angry. But what can I do to settle down?”

• Repeat affirmations or supportive phrases like, “This
is not the most fun or my best moment, but I’ll get
through it.”

• Visualize yourself settling down. Picture yourself by a
lake, tossing in rocks. As each rock falls, feel yourself relax
into your feet or chair.
On High Alert

• Try triangle breathing: Breath in four counts; hold for four
counts; exhale for six counts. Pause and repeat.

• Physically remove yourself from the stressor or
situation.
• Engage in physical activity (e.g., walk, run, or swim).

• Turn to a timed distraction. Listen to music or play a
game on your phone.

Use When You’re

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: pdf_to_json_text_layout_v1
ourspace_kindness_pass.category: ADHD Emotion Regulation Anger and Calming

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Use a timer, body double, or transition cue.
Stop after one tiny step if energy drops.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
